text.title: javascript Core Rules
text.summary: Nine testable rules covering TS strictness, exports, validation, components, hooks, layering, and tooling.

rule.statement: Enable strict: true plus noUncheckedIndexedAccess and exactOptionalPropertyTypes in tsconfig.json. Never use `any` in production code; use `unknown` instead.
rule.rationale: strict catches most null-deref bugs at compile time; any escapes the type system entirely.

rule.statement: Prefer named exports over default exports. Use default only for framework requirements (Next.js pages, React.lazy).
rule.rationale: Named exports enable accurate IDE refactoring + reliable tree-shaking + grep-friendly imports.

rule.statement: Validate external inputs at the boundary with zod/valibot; trust types only for internal data. Use discriminated unions for Result types.
rule.rationale: TS types disappear at runtime; runtime validation at the boundary is the only way to keep types honest.

rule.statement: Use function declarations (not arrow functions) for components. Define a Props interface. Return React.ReactElement, not JSX.Element or React.FC.
rule.rationale: function declarations show up in stack traces by name; React.FC implies children which is wrong by default.

rule.statement: Define an explicit interface for hook return types. Include error handling and cleanup. Use useCallback for memoized callbacks with dependency arrays.
rule.rationale: Explicit return types prevent accidental shape changes that ripple through consumers.

rule.statement: Always include a runtime guard in context consumer hooks. Throw a clear error if the context is missing.
rule.rationale: Silent undefined context propagates through children; explicit throw makes the bug local.

rule.statement: Controllers handle only HTTP (parse request, call service, send response). Services contain business logic and have NO HTTP imports. Repositories handle data access only.
rule.rationale: Layer isolation enables unit-testing services without a HTTP stack and swapping repositories without touching services.

rule.statement: Use Vitest for Node and browser tests. Configure jsdom for component tests.
rule.rationale: Vitest aligns with Vite ecosystem, supports ESM, and is faster than Jest for typical TS projects.

rule.statement: Use MSW (Mock Service Worker) for API mocking. Define handlers once; reuse in tests, Storybook, and browser dev. Prefer MSW over mocking fetch/axios directly.
rule.rationale: fetch-mocking couples tests to the HTTP client; MSW intercepts at the network layer so the same handlers cover all clients.

rule.statement: strict tsconfig, named exports, zod at boundaries, layered Node services, Vitest+MSW.
rule.rationale: Aggregate conclusion target for the positive branch.

rule.statement: TS 5 cannot be adopted, or strict is off-limits for this codebase.
rule.rationale: Aggregate conclusion target for the negative branch.
